coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained attention in near-infrared (NIR, 700–1700 nm) bioimaging and image-guided tumor phototherapy due to their outstanding optical properties and favorable biocompatibility. However, constructing CDs for NIR-II fluorescence imaging-guided phototherapy remains challenging. In this study, we present molecularly engineered NIR-II emitting carbon dots assemblies (NECDs-A) for advanced NIR-II bioimaging and synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT)/photothermal tumor therapy (PTT). By using terephthalaldehyde and pyrrole as specifically selected precursors, the resulting NECDs-A feature a large conjugated framework, with high graphitic nitrogen content and abundant surface carbonyl groups. These structural characteristics enable NECDs-A to exhibit NIR-II fluorescence emission, with a peak at 950 nm and a maximum wavelength extending beyond 1400 nm, achieving precise tumor visualization and high signal-to-noise ratio whole-body angiography. Additionally, the as-prepared NECDs-A demonstrate type-I photodynamic activity and a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 45.3 %) under 808 nm laser irradiation. In vitro and in vivo studies confirm their ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and heat, leading to efficient image-guided tumor eradication without noticeable toxicity. This work provides a new example of NIR-II emitting CDs design strategies and their application in tumor phototherapy guided by NIR-II imaging.

Synthesis and Characteristics of NECDs-A. a) Diagrammatic drawing of the synthesis of NECDs-A. b) & c) TEM photograph, d) Absorbance spectra and e) Fluorescence emission spectra of NECDs-A. f) Degradation curves of DPBF absorption, g) Increased FL curves of DCFH-DA and h) Increased FL curves of DHE in the presence of NECDs-A under continuous laser irradiation. i) ESR spectra of the NECDs-A with DMPO as O2 −• trapper, the laser wavelength used is 808 nm.

(b) Temperature variations of NECDs-A solution under 808nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2). c)The photothermal effect of NECDs-A aqueous solution under 808nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2) for 10 min) and cooling process. d) Plot of cooling time data vs −lnθ and the relative linear fitting curve obtained from the cooling stage. e) Thermal imaging of water and NECDS-A aqueous solution under 808 nm laser irradiation. f) Temperature variations of NECDs-A solution under 808nm irradiation (1 W/cm2) for four cycles. g) Temperature variations of NECDs-A solution and benzaldehyde-pyrrole CDs-A under 808nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2).

In Vivo Tumor Enrichment Imaging and Photothermal Effect Visualization. a) NIR-II fluorescence images of NECDs-A at various time points post-injection, along with b) corresponding quantitative analysis. c) Thermal images of the tumor region in tumor-bearing mice under 808 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2) at different time points, 48 h after injection of either PBS or NECDs-A.

In vivo tumor therapeutic proper ties of NECDs-A. a) Schematic illustration of the establishment of 4 T1 tumor bearing mice and evaluating the therapy efficiency of NECDs-A. b) Body weights changes of mice with different treatments in 21 days. c, d) The relative tumor growth curves of 4 T1 tumor bearing mice after various treatments. e) Optical Photos, f) H&E stained slices, g) Ki-67 immunohistochemistry and h) TUNEL (green)/DAPI (blue) immunofluorescence of tumor tissues with different treatments. PBS (Group I), 0.8 W/cm2 laser (Group II), NECDs-A (Group III), NECDs-A with 0.3 W/cm2 808 nm laser (Group IV), and NECDs-A with 0.8 W/cm2 808 nm laser (Group V), scale bar: 200 μm. Unpaired t-test analysis was used for statistical analysis (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 and ****P < 0.0001).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained attention in near-infrared (NIR, 700–1700 nm) bioimaging and image-guided tumor phototherapy due to their outstanding optical properties and favorable biocompatibility. However, constructing CDs for NIR-II fluorescence imaging-guided phototherapy remains challenging. In this study, we present molecularly engineered NIR-II emitting carbon dots assemblies (NECDs-A) for advanced NIR-II bioimaging and synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT)/photothermal tumor therapy (PTT). By using terephthalaldehyde and pyrrole as specifically selected precursors, the resulting NECDs-A feature a large conjugated framework, with high graphitic nitrogen content and abundant surface carbonyl groups. These structural characteristics enable NECDs-A to exhibit NIR-II fluorescence emission, with a peak at 950 nm and a maximum wavelength extending beyond 1400 nm, achieving precise tumor visualization and high signal-to-noise ratio whole-body angiography. Additionally, the as-prepared NECDs-A demonstrate type-I photodynamic activity and a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 45.3 %) under 808 nm laser irradiation. In vitro and in vivo studies confirm their ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and heat, leading to efficient image-guided tumor eradication without noticeable toxicity. This work provides a new example of NIR-II emitting CDs design strategies and their application in tumor phototherapy guided by NIR-II imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioimaging-guided tumor therapy has become prominent research areas in recent years, particularly fluorescence imaging and its associated phototherapy including photodynamic therapy (PDT)/photothermal tumor therapy (PTT) due to their intuitive and straightforward characteristics [1–3]. However, the absorption, scattering, and autofluorescence of biological tissues in the visible light range (400–700 nm) significantly impact the outcomes of fluorescence imaging and tumor phototherapy [4]. In contrast, the absorption, scattering, and autofluorescence of biological tissues in the near-infrared range (NIR 700–1700 nm) are negligible, which is advantageous for NIR fluorescence image guided tumor therapy while minimizing damage to normal tissues [5,6]. To date, numerous organic molecules have achieved NIR-II range (900–1700 nm) emission through strategies such as steric hindrance [7], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [8,9], and J-aggregation [10]. For example, Wu et al. accomplished bright NIR-II imaging and efficient photothermal tumor therapy by utilizing steric hindrance [7], while Wan et al. converted D-A molecules into J-aggregates through methyl modification, enabling whole-body vascular imaging and high-performance tumor phototherapy [10]. These examples clearly illustrate that the formation of large conjugated structures is one of the key factors underlying NIR-II emission [11]. Semiconductor quantum dots have also made significant progress in NIR-II imaging. For example, Hong et al. reported PEG-modified Ag2S quantum dots and applied them for NIR-II in vivo imaging, achieving excellent imaging results [12]. Despite significant progress made with organic molecules and semiconductor quantum dots, these materials still face limitations, including complex synthesis processes and the high costs, as well as the biosafety concerns and potential environmental hazards posed by heavy metals doped in semiconductor quantum dots. Therefore, the development of cost-effective and environmentally friendly NIR agents for fluorescence imaging-guided tumor phototherapy remains an urgent need.

Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials that have shown significant potential in fluorescence imaging guided tumor therapy due to their simple synthesis, ease of surface functionalization, and excellent photochemical-physical properties [13–19]. Despite numerous studies on CDs for bioimaging guided tumor phototherapy, CDs capable of NIR emission or NIR light triggered phototherapy remain in their initial stage [20–23]. For examples, Zhang et al. designed near-infrared absorbing CDs based on polaron engineering, facilitating NIR-II in vivo imaging and PTT [24]. Han et al. reported CDs with NIR-II emission in aqueous solutions through surface modification, enabling angiography imaging and inflammation site detection [25]. Additionally, NIR-CDs reported in recent years share some common characteristics, including frequent doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen and selenium, as well as a high content of carbonyl groups. These features regulate the electronic structure and energy level distribution of the CDs, promoting absorption and emission at longer wavelengths, thereby enhancing their effectiveness in applications such as bioimaging and image guided phototherapy [25,26]. However, CDs for NIR bioimaging guided cancer phototherapy still face several challenges: 1) most CDs exhibit emission peaks in the NIR-I region and require short-wavelength laser excitation, which would reduce the outcomes of bioimaging and phototherapy [27]. 2) Most of current NIR CDs are incapable for bioimaging and simultaneous phototherapy,especially for NIR-II imaging-guided phototherapy [28]. Therefore, developing CDs for NIR-II fluorescence imaging and simultaneous phototherapy could help overcome their current limitations.

In this work, inspired by the extended conjugation of NIR-II emitting organic molecules and the facile functionalization of CDs, we propose a novel molecular engineering strategy for synthesizing NIR-II emitting CDs with a large conjugated system, abundant carbonyl groups, and graphitic nitrogen. This is the first time that the as-prepared NIR-II emitting carbon dots assemblies (NECDs-A) have successfully integrated NIR-II emission, type-I PDT activity, and efficient photothermal conversion (Scheme 1 a). Specifically, under 808 nm laser excitation, NECDs-A exhibited NIR-II fluorescence with an emission peak at 950 nm and a maximum wavelength extending beyond 1400 nm. Moreover, NECDs-A exhibit type-I PDT activity and excellent photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 45.3%). In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated that NECDs-A generated significant amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and heat under 808 nm laser irradiation, leading to effective tumor cell killing without any noticeable dark toxicity. In vivo imaging results revealed high signal-to-noise ratio angiography of the whole body, and tumor therapy confirmed complete tumor eradication through combined PDT/PTT therapy, with no significant biotoxicity (Scheme 1b). This study presents a novel type of NECDs-A with NIR-II fluorescence emission, type-I PDT activity, and PTT through molecular engineering design, addressing some existing issues compared to previously reported works (Table S1), such as the complexity of organic molecule synthesis steps, high costs, potential biosafety concerns of semiconductor quantum dots, and the functional singularity of CDs, thereby providing a robust platform for efficient NIR-II bioimaging and advanced cancer therapy.

All chemical agents were used as received without further purification unless otherwise noted. 1,4-benzenedicarboxaldehyde, benzaldehyde, Pluronic F-127, Pyrrole and Trifluoroacetic acid were obtained from Innochem (Beijing, China). 3-(4-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenylte-trazolium bromide (MTT) assay kits, Calcein-AM/PI kits, Dihydroethidium(DHE), 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and 3′-p-(hydroxyphenyl) fluorescein (HPF) were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were obtained from Merck (Germany).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed on HT7700 and a JEM-2100 field-emission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on an Excalibur 3100 spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses (Thermal Instruments Inc., U.S.). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were obtained from FLS 1000 (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). NIR-II FL-images were acquired on a MARS NIR-II In-vivo Imaging System (Artemis Intelligent Imaging, China).

94 mg of 1,4-benzenedicarboxaldehyde was completely dissolved in 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol, followed by the addition of 1.0 mL of pyrrole and 150 μL of trifluoroacetic acid. After thorough mixing, the solution was transferred to a microwave reactor equipped with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner. The reaction was conducted according to the following program: 90°C for 5 min, 130°C for 3 min, 160°C for 10 min. After cooling to room temperature, NECDs were obtained. The benzaldehyde-pyrrole CDs exhibited results similar to those mentioned above, with the only change being the substitution of terephthalaldehyde with benzaldehyde.

The synthesis of NECDs-A followed a procedure similar to the synthesis of NECDs, with the exception that 3 g of F-127 was added. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled and then transferred to a dialysis bag. Dialysis was performed for 24 h to remove ethanol and unreacted materials. The benzaldehyde-pyrrole CDs exhibited results similar to those mentioned above, with the only change being the substitution of terephthalaldehyde with benzaldehyde.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield measurements of NECDs-A
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of NECDs-A was measured utilizing dye IR-26 as a reference, whose QY has been reported as 0.5% in 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) [29]. The QY value of NECDs-A was measured based on four concentrations with OD of 0.019, 0.042, 0.064 and 0.084 at 808 nm. The integrated total emission intensity was in the 900–1400 nm region. Two slopes, one obtained for the IR-26 reference and the other from NECDs-A, were used in the calculation of the quantum yield of NECDs-A in water, according to the following Eq. (1).

where ηsam and ηref are the refractive index of water and DCE, respectively.

DPBF was used to measure the ROS production of NECDs-A. The absorption of the NECDs-A at 808 nm was adjusted to approximately 0.20 OD. Then, 30 μL of DPBF solution (in DMF, 1 mg/mL) was added to the 2 mL NECDs-A solution, followed by irradiation with an 808 nm laser at 0.3 W/cm2. Data was collected every 1 min up to 15 min. The decrease in DPBF absorption over time was proportional to the production of ROS.

DCFH-DA was used to detect the ability of NECDs-A to produce ROS. With ROS production, DCFH-DA was oxidized and emitted fluorescence at ∼530 nm. The absorption of the NECDs-A at 808 nm was adjusted to approximately 0.20 OD. Then, 10 μL of DCFH-DA solution (in DMF, 10 mM) was added to the NECDs-A solution. The mixture was irradiated with an 808 nm laser at 0.3 W/cm2. A fluorescence spectrometer collected the fluorescence changes every minute for up to 10 min.

DHE was used to detect the ability of NECDs-A to produce O2 •−. With O2 •− production, it would oxidize DHE to form ethidium, which further reacted with DNA and emitted fluorescence at ∼605 nm. The absorption of the NECDs-A at 808 nm was adjusted to approximately 0.20 OD. Then, 10 μL of DHE solution (in DMSO, 10 mM) and 10 μL of ctDNA solution (PBS, 10 mg/mL) were added to the NECDs-A solution. The mixture was irradiated with an 808 nm laser at 0.3 W/cm2. A fluorescence spectrometer collected the fluorescence changes every minute for up to 10 min.

The generation of O2 •− was monitored using ESR spectroscopy with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping agent. When O2 •− was produced, it reacted with DMPO to form DMPO-OOH. The absorption of the NECDs-A in water at 808 nm was adjusted to approximately 0.20 OD. Then, 10 μL of DMPO (0.2 M) was added to the NECDs-A solution. The mixture was irradiated with an 808 nm laser at 0.3 W/cm2 for 10 min. The ESR spectrum was monitored within the range of 3460–3560 G to detect the spin signals.

To evaluate the photothermal performance, 1 mL of NECDs-A aqueous dispersion with various concentrations (0–100 μg/mL) in a quartz cuvette was irradiated by the 808 nm laser at 1 W/cm2 for 10 min [30]. The temperature changes of the NECDs-A solution (100 μg/mL) were also measured under the irradiation of the 808 nm laser at various power densities. All temperature changes were recorded using a Thermal Imager. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of NECDs-A was calculated using Eq. (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of O2 •- generation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cellswere seededin 35 mm confocal dishes andincubatedfor 24 h. After washing with PBS, the cellswere incubatedwith DMEM medium containing 100 μg/mL of NECDs-A at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, fresh mediumwas added, containing 50 μM DHE. After further incubation for 0.5 h, the cellswere irradiatedwith an 808 nm laser (0.3 W/cm2) for 10 min. The bright red fluorescencewas capturedusing a Nikon A1 laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 561 nm). In addition, the dark toxicity of NECDs-Awas evaluatedby following the above procedure without laser irradiation.

HeLa cellswere seededin 35 mm confocal dishes andincubatedfor 24 h. After washing with PBS, the cellswere incubatedwith DMEM medium containing 100 μg/mL of NECDs-A at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, fresh mediumwas added, containing 10 μM HPF. After incubation for 0.5 h, the cellswere irradiatedwith an 808 nm laser (0.3 W/cm2) for 10 min. The bright green fluorescencewas capturedusing a Nikon A1 laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 488 nm). In addition, the dark toxicity of NECDs-Awas evaluatedby following the above procedure without laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ROS generation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cellswere seededin 35 mm confocal dishes andincubatedfor 24 h. After washing with PBS, the cellswere incubatedwith DMEM medium containing 100 μg/mL of NECDs-A at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, fresh mediumwas added, containing 10 μM DCFH-DA. After incubation for 0.5 h, the cellswere irradiatedwith an 808 nm laser (0.3 W/cm2) for 10 min. The bright green fluorescencewas capturedusing a Nikon A1 laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 488 nm). In addition, the dark toxicity of NECDs-Awas evaluatedby following the above procedure without laser irradiation.

To assess the cytotoxicity of NECDs-A using the MTT assay, various cellswere seededinto a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h. After different treatments, the culture medium was removed, and 10% MTT solution (in medium)was addedandincubated for 2–4 h. Subsequently, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbancewas measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

To characterize the cytotoxic effects of different treatments on HeLa cells, approximately 105 HeLa cellswere first seededinto glass-bottomed culture dishes andincubatedfor 24 h. The cellswere then subjectedto the following treatment groups: no treatment, 808 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2), co-incubation with NECDs, co-incubation with NECDs followed by 10 min of 808 nm laser irradiation (0.3 W/cm2), and co-incubation with NECDs followed by 10 min of 808 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2). After replacing the medium with fresh culture medium, the cellswere incubatedovernight. Following medium removal, the cellswere co-stainedwith calcein AM and propidium iodide (PI) for 30 min. After washing with PBS three times, the cellswere observedusing a NIKON A1 Confocal microscope. Calcein AMenteredlive cells due to esterases andwas convertedto calcein (calcein green), whichemittedstrong green fluorescence upon excitation with a 488 nm laser, allowing detection of live cells. PIpenetratedthe compromised membranes of dead cells andintercalatedwith DNA, emitting red fluorescence upon excitation with a 561 nm laser, thereby identifying dead cells.

In vivo angiographywas performedusing 6 to 8-week-old Balb/c mice. Prior to imaging, the Balb/c micewere anesthetized. NECDs-A (100 µL, 1.00 mg/mL)was intravenously injectedinto the mice. The micewere then excitedwith an 808 nm laser, and NIR-II fluorescence imageswere acquiredusing a NIR camera equipped with long-pass filters ranging from 1000 to 1350 nm. No signs of toxicological effectswere observedin any of the mice during the experiment, and all micewere euthanizedat the end of the study.

In vivo tumor imagingwas conductedusing 6 to 8-week-old Balb/c mice. NECDs-A (100 µL, 1.00 mg/mL)was administeredvia tail vein injection into tumor-bearing mice. Fluorescence images of the micewere capturedat different time points. For organ distribution imaging, NECDs-Awas also administeredvia tail vein injection into tumor-bearing mice. Micewere euthanizedat the end, and organswere excisedfor imaging. The imagingwas performedusing a near-infrared imaging system with an excitation wavelength of 808 nm, and fluorescence imageswere collectedwith a 1000 LP filter. No signs of toxicological effectswere observedin any of the mice during the experiment, and all micewere euthanizedat the end of the study.

Photothermal imaging of tumorswas conductedusing 6 to 8-week-old Balb/c mice. NECDs-A (100 µL, 1.00 mg/mL)was administeredvia tail vein injection to tumor-bearing mice. After 48 h, the tumorswere irradiatedwith a laser (0.8 W/cm2), and thermal imageswere capturedat various time points using an infrared thermal imaging camera. No signs of toxicological effectswere observedin any of the mice during the experiment, and all micewere euthanizedat the end of the study.

A tumor-bearing mouse modelwas establishedby subcutaneously injecting 5 × 105 4T1 cells into the upper dorsal region of female BALB/c mice. Once the tumor volumereachedapproximately 150 mm3, the micewere randomly dividedinto five groups for tail vein injection of 100 μL of PBS or NECDs-A (n = 5 per group). The groupswereas follows: I, intravenous injection of PBS; II, intravenous injection of PBS + 0.8 W/cm2 808 nm laser; III, intravenous injection of NECDs-A; IV, intravenous injection of PBS + 808 nm laser (0.3 W/cm2); V, intravenous injection of NECDs-A + 808 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2). Tumor volumeswere recordedevery 2 days using Eq. (3).

All mice were euthanized on day 23 after the initial treatment.

For histological analysis, the major organs of mice (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) are fixed in tissue fixative and then embedded in paraffin. The organ sections are stained with H&E and subjected to immunohistochemical staining. Histological sections are imaged using an optical microscope. For blood analysis, serum and plasma are collected from mice at the end of the in vivo experiments and analyzed for complete blood count and liver and kidney function.

The related data were representative results from at least three independent experiments and the statistical analyses were carried out by GraphPad Prism Software. Comparison between two groups was performed by Student's t-test. Comparison among multiple groups was performed by one-way ANOVA, and further comparison was performed by LSD-t test. *P < 0.05 indicated that the difference was statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characteristics of NECDs and NECDs-A
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of NECDs was accomplished via a solvothermal reaction utilizing a microwave reactor. Specifically, 1,4-benzenedicarboxaldehyde and pyrrole were dissolved in ethanol and subjected to microwave irradiation under acidic conditions for 10 min. The schematic representation of the synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 a. During this process, the carbonyl group of terephthalaldehyde reacts with pyrrole under acidic and heated conditions, leading to further cross-linking and condensation to form a porphyrin-like conjugated structure. Due to the aldehyde groups at both ends of the terephthalaldehyde, this conjugated structure continues to expand, resulting in an extended conjugated framework. At elevated temperatures, the system undergoes carbonization, culminating in an aromatic, large conjugated structure. Initially, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images reveal that the NECDs are uniformly sized, with an average diameter of approximately 10 nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) indicates that the NECDs exhibit an amorphous structure (Fig. 1b). The presence of amorphous structural defects and various heteroatoms in amorphous carbon materials results in a less defined band structure [31]. These defects and heteroatoms introduce additional defect states, reducing the effective bandgap of the material and consequently leading to emission at longer wavelengths. As a result, the absorption spectrum shows that the NECDs exhibit a distinct absorption peak in the near-infrared region, with the main peak located at 810 nm (Fig. 1c). When excited by an 808 nm laser, they display NIR-II fluorescence with an emission peak at 950 nm and a maximum wavelength extending beyond 1400 nm (Fig. 1d). To validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, multiple batches of synthesis were conducted, showing no significant differences in their absorption spectra (Fig. S1). Additionally, quantitative analysis of yield and production rate across batches revealed no notable variations, demonstrating the strategy's reliability and reproducibility (Table S2 and Fig. S2). To further corroborate this approach, benzaldehyde was used as a substitute for p-phthalaldehyde during synthesis. Owing to the presence of only a single aldehyde group, the resulting system failed to achieve the desired conjugation expansion. As a result, the benzaldehyde-pyrrole CDs exhibited significantly diminished absorption in the NIR region (Fig. 1e) and a fivefold reduction in NIR-II fluorescence intensity compared to NECDs (Fig. 1f).

To gain a deeper understanding of the exceptional optical properties of NECDs, further characterization was conducted to reveal their microstructure. Raman spectroscopy analysis showed characteristic peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1436 cm−1, corresponding to the sp2 (G band) and sp3 (D band) domains, respectively (Fig. 1g). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of NECDs (Fig. 1h). The peak at 3328 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of N-H, while the band at 2843 cm−1 corresponds to –CH2 groups. The peak around 1650 cm−1 is attributed to C=N/C=C bonds. These findings suggest the presence of a conjugated aromatic structure, along with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups [32,33].

The chemical composition of the prepared NECDs was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1i, the full-scale XPS spectra demonstrate that the NECDs consist of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements with atomic ratios of 85.23%, 9.90%, and 3.09%, respectively (Table S3). The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. S3a) reveal the presence of C=C carbon (284.7 eV), C=N carbon (286.0 eV), and C=O groups (291.5 eV), providing solid evidence for the existence of a large conjugated system [32]. The high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. S3b) feature two peaks corresponding to pyrrolic nitrogen (399.3 eV, 58.6%) and graphitic nitrogen (400.3 eV. 41.4%). The presence of pyrrolic nitrogen indicates the incorporation of nitrogen into the conjugated structure, facilitating electronic transitions and enhancing the photophysical properties. Meanwhile, graphitic nitrogen may contribute to the stability and electronic delocalization within the carbon framework [34,35]. The O1s band (Fig. S3c) can be deconvoluted into two peaks located at 531.9 eV and 532.5 eV, representing the C=O and C−O groups. Notably, the high content of C=O (92.7%) also contributes to the longer absorption and emission spectra of NECDs [26,36].

Overall, the NECDs were synthesized through a microwave-assisted solvothermal reaction involving terephthalaldehyde and pyrrole under acidic conditions, resulting in a large conjugated structure with significant graphitic nitrogen content. Characterization techniques revealed that the NECDs exhibit uniform size, an amorphous structure. The presence of various functional groups and structural features, such as high nitrogen content and significant carbonyl groups, contributed to the remarkable NIR-II fluorescence emission, with an emission peak at 950 nm and a maximum wavelength extending beyond 1400 nm.

To address the challenge of NECDs' inability to disperse in physiological environments, the amphiphilic molecule F127 was employed to modify the NECDs, as detailed in Fig. 2 a. During the synthesis, F127 was incorporated, resulting in the formation of NECDs-Assemblies following dialysis. The NECDs-A, shown in Fig. 2b, have an average diameter of approximately 200 nm. High-magnification images confirm the distribution of NECDs within the NECDs-A (Fig. 2c). The absorption spectrum of the assemblies shows significant absorption between 700 nm and 900 nm, with a prominent peak around 810 nm (Fig. 2d), akin to the absorption profile of NECDs (Fig. 1d). The absence of significant differences in absorption values across different batches indicates the effectiveness of the strategy (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). Additionally, the emission spectrum of NECDs-A spans 900 to 1400 nm, with a primary peak near 950 nm (Fig. 2e). Compared to the emission profile of NECDs (Fig. 1e), NECDs-A exhibits a notable shoulder peak around 1000 nm, which may be indicative of aggregation-induced redshift in the NECDs-A [37]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield of NECDs-A was determined using the comparative method, with IR26 serving as the reference, which has a reported quantum yield of 0.5% [29,38]. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence quantum yield of NECDs-A was calculated to be 0.61%, which is 1.2 times higher than that of IR26. This result highlights the superior fluorescence performance of NECDs-A under 808 nm laser excitation.

The photodynamic mechanism of NECDs-A is primarily attributed to its molecularly engineered structure, including a large conjugated system, high graphitic nitrogen content, and abundant carbonyl groups, which collectively enhance intersystem crossing and facilitate efficient ROS generation under 808 nm laser irradiation. To validate this mechanism and quantitatively assess ROS production, a series of experiments were conducted. Initially, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a ROS detection probe to monitor changes in absorbance at 416 nm. As shown in Fig. 2f, the absorbance of DPBF progressively decreased after mixing with NECDs-A, indicating that NECDs-A effectively generate ROS under 808 nm laser irradiation. In addition to the DPBF absorbance measurements, we also assessed the fluorescence intensity of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), a widely used indicator for ROS detection. After mixing with NECDs-A, the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA significantly increased over the duration of 808 nm laser irradiation, as depicted in Fig. 2g. This increase in fluorescence intensity further corroborates the production of ROS, aligning with the results obtained from the DPBF assays. To identify the specific types of ROS produced, we utilized dihydroethidium (DHE) as a selective probe for O2 •−. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of DHE increased with prolonged 808 nm laser irradiation after mixing with NECDs-A, indicating a significant generation of superoxide anions over time (Fig. 2h). To further validate these findings, complementary electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was conducted. The EPR spectra displayed a characteristic 1:1:1:1 peak pattern (Fig. 2i), which is indicative of the presence of O2 •−. This confirmation indicates that the ROS generated by NECDs-A under 808 nm laser irradiation are indeed O2 •−, thereby demonstrating that NECDs-A functions as a type I photosensitizer. Together, these findings highlight the effectiveness of NECDs-A in ROS generation under 808 nm laser excitation and underscore their potential applications in PDT.

The structure of NECDs-A, characterized by an extensive conjugated system and high graphitic nitrogen content, enhances its ability to absorb NIR light and convert it into heat efficiently. This is achieved by promoting non-radiative relaxation pathways, where absorbed energy is dissipated as heat. These structural features, coupled with the superior spectral absorption properties of NECDs-A, prompted an investigation into their potential as a NIR-responsive PTT agent. As the power of the 808 nm laser was increased from 0.2 W/cm2 to 1.0 W/cm2, a rapid rise in the temperature of NECDs-A was observed (Fig. 3 a). Under 1 W/cm2 of 808 nm laser irradiation, the temperature of NECDs-A increased from room temperature (25°C) to 65°C. Additional studies revealed a clear concentration-dependent effect on the photothermal performance of NECDs-A. As a control, the temperature increase of water was only 2°C, whereas NECDs-A reached 56°C at a concentration of 50 μg/mL and 65°C at a concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 3b and 3e). To further quantify the photothermal conversion efficiency (η), the temperature changes of NECDs-A (100 μg/mL, 1 mL) were recorded during the heating and cooling cycles (Fig. 3c). Analysis of the cooling phase data demonstrated that the photothermal conversion efficiency of NECDs-A was caclulated to be 45.3% (Fig. 3d; comprehensive calculations are delineated in the experimental section). Moreover, NECDs-A preserved the maximum photothermal temperature with negligible attenuation after four cycles, indicative of their exceptional photothermal stability (Fig. 3f). As a control, carbon dots prepared from benzaldehyde and pyrrole exhibited only a slight temperature increase (Fig. 3g). These findings imply that NECDs-A represent highly promising candidates for PTT.

The generation of ROS and the cytotoxic effects of NECDs-A toward tumor cells were further investigated to provide insights into their photodynamic mechanisms and therapeutic efficacy. Initially, the type I photosensitizing activity of NECDs-A under 808 nm laser irradiation was evaluated using DHE as a selective probe for O2 •−. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the red fluorescence of DHE remained unchanged in the PBS treatment group, even with laser exposure. In contrast, NECDs-A combined with 808 nm laser irradiation induced a significant enhancement in DHE fluorescence with prolonged light exposure, demonstrating effective O2 •− generation through type I photodynamic activity. These O2 •− molecules can subsequently be converted into hydroxyl radicals (•OH) via the Haber-Weiss reaction, a critical step in amplifying oxidative damage [39]. The HPF probe, specific for •OH, exhibited a substantial increase in fluorescence, confirming the conversion of O2 •− into more reactive •OH species (Fig. S7). Concurrently, a marked elevation in DCFH-DA fluorescence indicated significant ROS accumulation within tumor cells (Fig. 4b). These results comprehensively highlight the robust ROS generation capability of NECDs-A under 808 nm laser, establishing a strong basis for their application in cytotoxicity assays and further advancing their potential in tumor PTT, laying a strong foundation for subsequent cytotoxicity assays and in vivo applications.

The cytotoxicity of NECDs-A was evaluated using the MTT assay. Initially, the toxicity of NECDs-A towards normal cells was assessed by co-incubating varying concentrations of NECDs-A with HUEVC for 48 h. As shown in Fig. 4c, even at higher concentrations (250 μg/mL), NECDs-A exhibited minimal cytotoxicity towards normal cells. In the dark toxicity experiments, different concentrations of NECDs-A were co-incubated with HeLa for 48 h, revealing no significant dark toxicity of NECDs-A towards the HeLa cells (Fig. 4d). Subsequently, NECDs-A at different concentrations were co-incubated with HeLa, followed by irradiation with 0.3 W/cm2 of 808 nm laser, Fig. 4e shows that as the concentration of NECDs-A increased, the cytotoxicity to cells also increased. At 100 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was approximately 50%, with this cytotoxic effect attributed to the type I photodynamic activity of NECDs-A under the low laser power. When the laser power was increased to 0.8 W/cm2, due to the enhanced photothermal effect and photodynamic activity of NECDs-A, the survival rate of HeLa cells decreased to below 5% (Fig. 4f). These results highlight the excellent photosensitizing activity and cytotoxicity of NECDs-A. Further evaluation of the cancer cell-killing effect of NECDs-A was performed using a Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) co-staining assay. As illustrated in Fig. 4g, the control group, laser-only group, and NECDs-A-only treatment group displayed robust green fluorescence, indicating minimal cell death. In the NECDs-A and 0.3 W/cm2 laser co-treatment group, both green and red fluorescence were observed, indicating partial cell death. Conversely, the NECDs-A and 0.8 W/cm2 laser co-treatment group predominantly exhibited red fluorescence, signifying extensive cancer cell lethality. These findings align with the MTT assay results and highlight the superior tumor cell-killing capability of NECDs-A, emphasizing their substantial potential for in vivo tumor treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo NIR-II fluorescence imaging and photothermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the NIR-II fluorescence imaging performance of NECDs-A, whole-body vascular visualization was conducted by intravenous injection of NECDs-A. Fluorescence signals within the 1000–1350 nm range were captured using a series of long-pass (LP) filters to assess high-resolution imaging at extended wavelengths in the NIR-II window. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , vascular structures were clearly visualized in all NIR-II images obtained with different LP filters. Remarkably, as the LP filter wavelength increased from 1000 nm to 1350 nm, the image contrast significantly improved, and background noise was markedly reduced. This enhancement is attributed to the strong NIR-II fluorescence of NECDs-A (Fig. S8). Furthermore, the cross-sectional intensity profile of a specific blood vessel in the hind limb, marked by the yellow line, was analyzed using Gaussian fitting. The fitting coefficients for the 1000, 1100, 1200, 1300, and 1350 nm LP filters were 0.41, 0.62, 0.82, 0.91, and 0.98, respectively, indicating an increasing signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) with longer wavelengths. Notably, the 1350 nm LP filter achieved the highest SNR of 3.93, underscoring the substantial potential of NECDs-A for high-resolution NIR-II imaging at longer wavelengths.

The excellent therapeutic efficacy of NECDs-A in vitro, along with its outstanding NIR-II imaging performance, prompted us to evaluate its in vivo biodistribution and tumor targeting using NIR-II fluorescence imaging. In this study, 4T1 tumor-bearing Balb/c nude mice were subjected to tail vein injection of NECDs-A, and imaging was performed before and after injection. As shown in Fig. 6 a, no fluorescence signal was detected prior to injection. Continuous monitoring after the injection of NECDs-A revealed a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity at the tumor site, peaking at 48 h (Fig. 6b), indicating that this time point is optimal for therapeutic intervention. Fluorescence imaging of mouse main organs at the 72-hour time point (Fig. S9) and ex vivo analysis further confirmed the significant accumulation of NECDs-A in the tumor.

Furthermore, post intravenous injection of 48 h, in vivo photothermal imaging of NECDs-A was conducted using a thermal camera. As depicted in Fig. 6c, mice administered NECDs-A exhibited a rapid temperature elevation at the tumor site under 808 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2), with temperatures escalating from approximately 36°C to 55°C. In contrast, tumors in mice not treated with NECDs-A experienced only a modest temperature increase to approximately 39.5°C under the same irradiation conditions. These findings underscore the potential of NECDs-A as an efficient PTT agent.

Encouraged by the promising in vivo imaging of NECDs-A, further in vivo therapeutic experiments were conducted. A 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model was established by subcutaneously implanting cancer cells (Fig. 7 a). When the tumor volume reached approximately 150 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into five groups: PBS as the control group (Group I), 0.8 W/cm2 laser (Group II), NECDs-A (Group III), NECDs-A with 0.3 W/cm2 808 nm laser (Group IV), and NECDs-A with 0.8 W/cm2 808 nm laser (Group V). After tail vein injection of NECDs-A, phototherapy was administered at 48 h.

Subsequently, the tumor volumes and body weights of mice in each group were recorded every 2 days over a three-week period (Fig. 7b). Changes in tumor volume were meticulously documented. As illustrated in Fig. 7c and 7d, tumors in Group IV demonstrated significantly attenuated growth compared to Groups I, II, and III. Notably, tumors in Group V were completely eradicated, with tumor weight trends consistent with tumor volume changes (Fig. S10a) and a marked improvement in survival rates (Fig. S10b). This observation suggests that 0.3W/cm2 808 nm laser irradiation is insufficient for complete tumor ablation. Conversely, escalating the 808 nm laser power markedly enhances both PDT and PTT, resulting in total tumor eradicated (Fig. 7e). Histological examination of tumor sections stained with H&E revealed that tumors in Group IV exhibited only partial necrosis, whereas those in Group V were entirely compromised (Fig. 7f). Additionally, Ki67 staining, a marker of cellular proliferation, corroborated this trend, as partial cellular proliferation was observed in Group IV, while no proliferative characteristics were detected in Group V (Fig. 7g). TUNEL immunofluorescence staining indicated a significantly elevated level of positive expression (green fluorescence) in Group IV and Group V relative to other groups (Fig. 7h). The exceptional antitumor efficacy of NECDs-A can be attributed to their remarkable performance in image-guided PDT and PTT. Compared to previously reported materials, NECDs-A represent a significant advancement in the field of carbon dots, particularly due to their synergistic effects in both PDT and PTT. While earlier studies in NIR-II field have shown promising results with each modality individually (Table S1), the combination of both in NECDs-A results in substantially enhanced therapeutic outcomes.

Throughout the NECDs-A treatment regimen, no significant weight loss was observed in the subjects (Fig. 7b). Following treatment, biocompatibility was further assessed through tissue biopsies and hematological analyses. H&E staining of major organs—including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys—revealed no discernible morphological changes (Fig. S11). Moreover, comprehensive hematological and biochemical assays conducted post-treatment indicated that NECDs-A and laser exposure did not induce significant infection or inflammation, nor did they adversely affect hepatic or renal function (Figs. S12 and S13). These findings demonstrate the low biotoxicity and high biocompatibility of NECDs-A.

In summary, through molecular engineering method, we successfully synthesized NECDs-A, exhibiting NIR-II fluorescence emission, type I photodynamic activity, and excellent photothermal conversion efficiency for high-resolution angiography and synergistic PDT/PTT. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, NECDs-A exhibited NIR-II fluorescence, generated ROS through type I PDT, and achieved a photothermal conversion efficiency of 45.3% with remarkable stability, attributed to their large conjugated structure, high graphite nitrogen content, and abundant carbonyl groups. In vitro experiments confirmed their effectiveness in killing tumor cells through PDT and PTT, while in vivo studies validated their role as agents for whole-body angiography and fluorescence image-guided tumor phototherapy, achieving the complete tumor eradication without observable side effects. This work provides a new design strategy for NIR-II emitting CDs and their application in tumor phototherapy.
